elements.sent-2411-003: A prototypical example of such a theorem is Kodaira's vanishing theorem, which states that if $A$ is an ample divisor on a smooth complex projective variety $X$, then \begin{equation}H^i(X,\OO_X(K_X+A))=0\end{equation} for all $i>0$.

elements.section-2411-002: Overview on the basics of toric geometry
elements.sent-2411-022: In the present section we recall notation, basic definitions and some theorems from the classical theory of complex toric varieties, with an emphasis on sheaf cohomology and positivity.
elements.sent-2411-023: Our reference for this material is \cite{CLS11 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: An $n$-dimensional torus is an algebraic group $T$ which is isomorphic to $(\mathbb{C ^*)^n$.}
elements.sent-2411-026: A character of a torus $T$ is a morphism $\chi\colon T\to\mathbb{C ^*$ of algebraic groups.}
elements.sent-2411-027: The set $M$ of characters of an $n$-dimensional torus is a free abelian group of rank $n$, that is, it constitutes a lattice.
elements.sent-2411-028: A one-parameter subgroup of a torus $T$ is a morphism $\lambda\colon \mathbb{C ^*\to T$ of algebraic groups.}
elements.sent-2411-029: The set $N$ of one-parameter subgroups of an $n$-dimensional torus is also a lattice.
